The document specifies a new Layer 2 service called Ethernet VPN EVPN).

For scenarios requiring Ethernet multipoint connectivity, MPLS deployments leverage Virtual Private LAN Services (VPLS) [RFC4761, RFC4762]. A VPLS service is built with a full-mesh of Ethernet pseudowires among MPLS Provider Edge (PE) routers that are part of a given layer-2 broadcast domain. A VPLS PE emulates an Ethernet bridge by performing data-plane MAC learning (also known as "flood and learn" operation) against traffic arriving over the pseudowires and local interfaces. Existing VPLS PE does support only single-home mode.

The new Layer 2 service Ethernet VPN (EVPN) [RFC7209], [RFC7432bis] is a next generation solution that provides Ethernet multipoint services over MPLS networks. An EVPN comprises of customer edge (CE) devices that are connected to provider edge (PE) devices, which form the edge of the MPLS infrastructure. A CE device can be a host, a router, or a switch. The PE devices provide Layer 2 virtual bridge connectivity between the CE devices. A CE device can be connected to a few PE devices. There can be multiple EVPNs in the provider network. Learning between the PE routers occurs in the control plane using BGP, unlike traditional bridging, where learning occurs in the data plane. Additionally, to transparent bridging EVPN supports VLAN-Aware bridging.

An EVI represents a VPN on a PE router. It serves the same role of an IP VPN Routing and Forwarding (VRF), and EVIs are assigned import / export Route Targets (RTs). Depending on the service multiplexing behaviors at the User to Network Interface (UNI), all traffic on a port (all-to-one bundling), or traffic on a VLAN (one-to-one mapping), or traffic on a list / range of VLANs (selective bundling) can be mapped to a Bridge Domain (BD). This BD is then associated to an EVI for forwarding towards the MPLS core.
The PE EVPN Instance ([RFC7432bis], chapter 6.4) includes the following:
We can differ the following EVPN models:
Table 1: EVPN models
The standard ([RFC7432bis]), chapters 6.1 - 6.3.1)) defines a few Service Interface types. The types specify customer service provided by PEs.
The following service types must be supported (see the CLI command service-type):
Table 2: EVPN Service Interface

Ethernet Segments are used to represent "site" connections. They are associated with the access-facing interfaces (physical or logical (LAG, sub-interface)) that they represent and are assigned a unique identifier (referred to as Ethernet Segment Identifier (ESI)). Furthermore, a site can be connected to one or more PEs attached to a common MPLS core to provide access redundancy. With this, the solutions can support PE geo-redundancy and multi-homing (beyond dual homing) requirements. Lastly, a site can be used to connect to a single device or to an access network.

An Ethernet Tag ID is a 32-bit field containing either a 12-bit or 24-bit identifier that identifies a particular broadcast domain (e.g., a VLAN) in an EVPN instance. The 12-bit identifier is called the VLAN ID (VID). An EVPN instance consists of one or more broadcast domains (one or more VLANs). VLANs are assigned to a given EVPN instance by the provider of the EVPN service. A given VLAN can itself be represented by multiple VIDs. In such cases, the PEs participating in that VLAN for a given EVPN instance are responsible for performing VLAN ID translation to/from locally attached CE devices.
The following scenarios are available:
For MHD EVPN defines two load balancing modes of operation:
Figure 1: All-Active and Single-Active Load Balancing

EVPN PEs signal and learn MAC addresses over the core via BGP. For this purpose, a new address-family in Multi-Protocol BGP (MP-BGP) and new BGP extended communities allow PE routers to advertise and learn prefixes that identify MAC addresses and Ethernet segments over the network. This contrasts with existing VPLS solutions, which rely solely on data-plane learning. Control-plane learning brings a number of benefits that allow EVPN to address the VPLS shortcomings listed previously, including support for multi-homing with per-flow load balancing. Finally, by using BGP as a common VPN control-plane, providers can now leverage their operational experience and scalability characteristics inherent to IP VPNs, for their Ethernet offerings.
A total of four new BGP routes have been defined in order to collectively achieve the following functions:

When used in conjunction with an MPLS data-plane, these BGP routes also signal the MPLS labels associated with MAC addresses and Ethernet segments. This separates the new technologies from existing L2VPN solutions. EVPN no longer require the signaling and maintenance of pseudowires. EVPN MPLS labels represent Multipoint-to-Point (MP2P) Label Switched Paths (LSPs). Similar to IP VPNs, an EVPN MPLS label is signaled for use by the rest of the PEs in the VPN. There is no longer a requirement to signal separate point-to-point pseudowire Virtual Circuit (VC) labels for each remote PE. This brings the scale of L2VPNs to the same level as IP VPNs.
At startup, PEs exchange EVPN routes to advertise the following:

After the initial EVPN route exchange, and when customer traffic arrives at the PE, EVPN MAC advertisement routes distribute reachability information over the core for each customer MAC address learned on local Ethernet segments. Each EVPN MAC route announces the customer MAC address, and the Ethernet segment associated with the port where the MAC was learned from, as well as an MPLS label. This EVPN MPLS label will be used later by remote PEs when sending traffic destined to the advertised MAC address.
Figure 2: EVPN Operations

Following [RFC7432bis], chapter 7.9, the system must automatically generate the following RD for an EVPN instance:
The implementation must support the Route Prioritization specified in ([RFC7432bis], chapter 7.12).
Configuration and advertising of local MAC addresses MUST be supported.

The implementation must support Data-Plane MAC learning by software on interfaces attached to local CEs ([RFC7432bis, 14.2.1). The Control-Plane learning on these interfaces ([RFC7432bis, 14.2.2) is not supported.
MAC learning from the LACP messages is not permitted.
The Remote MAC learning (see [RFC7432bis], chapter 9.2) MUST be supported.

A Multi-Homed Ethernet Segment ([RFC7432bis), chapter 5)) can be configurated on a LAG interface. The LAG must be a LACP LAG and the same LACP System MAC address and LACP Administrative Key must be configured in all PEs connected to this Ethernet Segment (see CLI commands lacp-system-mac and lacp-key).
Additionally, an ES can be configured on a Single-Homed interface (physical ethernet port or LAG). For this ES:
The following ESI types must be supported (see CLI commands identifier):
The ESI type specifies whether the ES is single home or multi-homing ethernet interface:
The implementation must support the following algorithms of Designated Forwarder Election (see command service-carving):

The algorithm configured by command service-carving is advertised in the Ethernet Segment Route (4), DF Election Extended Community, field 'DF Alg'. If the router receives at least one advertisement without the DF Election Extended Community or with a value of 'DF Alg' differing from its advertised value, the router must fall back to the Default Algorithm defined in [RFC7432bis].
The Signaling Primary and Backup DF Elected PEs (see [RFC7432bis], chapter 8.6) MUST be supported.
The Aliasing path (see [RFC7432bis], chapter 8.4) MUST be supported.
The Single-Active and ALL-Active modes and the Load Balancing of Unicast Packets (see [RFC7432bis], chapter 14) MUST be supported.
The MAC Mobility (see [RFC7432bis], chapter 15) MUST be supported.
The Fast Convergence (see [RFC7432bis], chapter 8.2) MUST be supported.
The Split Horizon (see [RFC7432bis], chapter 8.3) MUST be supported.
The multihoming procedures MUST be supported even by single-homing PEs (see [RFC7432bis], chapter 8.7). The document replaces SHOULD to MUST in the second rule into [RFC7432bis], chapters 8.7:

For single-homing PEs, all the above multihoming procedures can be omitted; however, to allow for single-homing PEs to fully interoperate with multihoming PEs, some of the multihoming procedures described above MUST be supported even by single-homing PEs.
The Best Path Selection algorithm for EVPN routes (see [RFC7432bis], chapter 7.13) must be supported.
Forwarding Unicast packets (see [RFC7432bis], chapter 13) MUST be supported.
Only Ingress Replication is supported, P2MP or MP2MP LSPs are not supported.
The document replaces SHOULD to MUST in the second rule in [RFC7432bis], chapters 8.3.1.1:

The Inclusive Multicast Ethernet Tag Routes must include P-Multicast Service Interface Tunnel (PMSI Tunnel) attribute ([RFC6514], chapter 5). In the case of Ingress Replication, the attribute must contain the following values:

If at least, one received Ethernet A-D Per EVPN Instance Route does not include the L2-Attr Extended Community, then the [RFC7432], chapter 14.1.1 is applied, otherwise [RFC7432bis], chapter 14.1.1 is applied.
The value of the ES-Import Route Target Extended community ([RFC7432bis], chapters 7.6) carried into an EVPN Ethernet Segment Route is set to the high-order 6-octet ESI Value.

Table 5: EVPN Routes

Each EVPN instance must advertise these routes for each of its ES.
The route contains the following fields:
Note. The value of ET is VLAN-ID in the case of VLAN-aware Bundle service type and 0 in the other cases.
Label LT2 specifies the sub-interface configured on an ES in the case of ac-based or the ES itself in the other cases.
Each EVPN instance must advertise these routes for its ES and each attached to it BD.

Note. The Ethernet A-D per EVI route may be received by a remote PE before it receives the set of Ethernet A-D per ES routes. Therefore, in order to handle corner cases and race conditions, the Ethernet A-D per EVI route MUST NOT be used for traffic forwarding by a remote PE until it also receives the associated set of Ethernet A-D per ED routes ([RFC7432bis], chapter 8.4).
The route contains the following fields:

Each EVPN instance must advertise these routes for each of its locally learned MAC address. The MPLS labels LT1 of MAC addresses assigned by this system and located into the same MACT (learnt into the same VPN) have the same value, but these labels LT1 in the received routes may have different values.
The route contains the following fields:
Each EVPN instance must advertise these routes for each of its Broadcast Domain.
The route contains the following fields:
A PE advertises these routes for each its ES.
If the DF Election Extended Community is omitted, the Default DF election algorithm is assumed.
The ES-Import Route Target Extended Community is used instead of the RT Extended Community in the import/export operations.
This document defines the following types of EVPN MPLS labels:
The following table summarizes information about EVPN labels:
Table 6: EVPN MPLS Label Types
Each EVPN instance creates an LT1 label for each of its MACT and distributes it into MAC/IP Address Advertisement Routes ([RFC7432], chapter 7.2).
The label specifies the MACT.
If a known unicast packet is forwarded to a remote PE that has advertised the MAC/IP Address Advertisement route with label LT1, the label LT1 is added to the packet ([RFC7432bis], chapter 8.4).
In an input PE, an LT1 label specifies the MACT used to forward this frame.
Each EVNP instance creates an LT2 label for each ES and distributes it into Ethernet A-D Route per EVPN Instance Routes ([RFC7432bis], chapter 7.1).
If a known unicast packet is forwarded to a remote PE that has not advertised the MAC/IP Address Advertisement route with label LT1, the LT2 label is added to the packet ([RFC7432bis], chapter 8.4).
In an input PE a LT2 label specifies the outgoing ES and ET (sub-interface if ET != 0).
Each EVNP instance creates an LT3 label for each BD and distributes it into Inclusive Multicast Ethernet Tag Routes (in the PMSI Tunnel attribute) ([RFC7432bis], chapter 7.3).
The label specifies the EVNP instance's non-ES interfaces and ES interfaces on which the PE is the DF.
When a PE forwards a BUM message via MPLS it adds the label if the remote PE is not the DF on the input ES.

Each EVPN instance creates an LT4 label (ESI label) for each its ES and distributes it into Ethernet A-D Route per Ethernet Segment Routes (in ESI Label Extended Community) ([RFC7432bis], chapter 7.1).
When non-DF PE forwards a BUM message to the remote DF PE it adds the LT4 label after the LT3 label.
The chapter describes the EVPN Forwarding Tables.
Each EVPN instance creates one MAC Unicast Forwarding Table (MACT). The table is used for forwarding known unicast MAC frames with known Destination MAC addresses is selected by
Each entry of these tables contains the following fields:
The following EVPN Routes are used to manage MACTs:
Each EVPN instance creates one BUM Split Horizon Table - one entry for each interface configured on an Ethernet segment.
The entry of the table contains the following fields:
The table is used for forwarding with Split Horizon BUM MAC frames arriving from remote PEs:
The frame is flooded to all local interfaces specified by the LT3 label except interface specified by the LT4 label.
The following EVPN Routes are used for this table management:
Each EVPN instance creates two BUM Forwarding Tables: one for input local traffic LBUMT) and one for input remote traffic (RBUMT).
The following table specifies the VLAN-ID value:
Table 7: VLAN-ID value
Note. In the case of ac-based service type this label specifies this sub-interface, in all other cases it specifies this ES (the LAG).

Only the higher layers are responsible for managing (adding, updating and deleting entries) the MAC Address Tables.
BCMRM together with BCM must only detect the needed cases and send the messages to the CPM without changing the MAC Address tables.

These MAC addresses are configured by a user.
These MAC addresses are learned from the local traffic.
These MAC addresses are received via the EVPN Control plan.
The MAC address type is received by BCMRM into the "MAC Address Add/Update" from a higher level.
The time aging must be applied only to local dynamic MAC addresses.

tables: The list of BGP common commands supported in the L2VPN EVPN context was added (chapter 2.2).
MACT with key MAC only (VLAN-ID = 0) and one BD
MACT with key <non-zero VLAN-ID, MAC> and a few BDs
Note: This document attempts to use VLAN-ID instead of ET-ID.

When all PEs attached to an Ethernet segment are allowed to forward known unicast traffic to/from that Ethernet segment for a given VLAN, then the Ethernet segment is defined to be operating in All-Active redundancy mode.

Broadcast Domain. In a bridged network, the broadcast domain corresponds to a Virtual LAN (VLAN), where a VLAN is typically represented by a single VLAN ID (VID) but can be represented by several VIDs where Shared VLAN Learning (SVL) is used per [IEEE.802.1Q_2014].
Bridge Table. An instantiation of a Bridge Forwarding Table on a MAC-VRF.
Customer Edge device. A CE device can be host, switch, or router.
Ethernet Segment. When a customer site (device or network) is connected to one or more PEs via a set of Ethernet links, then that set of links is referred to as an 'Ethernet segment'.
Ethernet Segment Identifier. A unique non-zero identifier that identifies an Ethernet segment is called an 'Ethernet Segment Identifier'.

Ethernet Tag. Used to represent a BD that is configured on a given ES for the purposes of DF election and <EVI, BD> identification for frames received from the CE. Note that any of the following may be used to represent a BD: VIDs (including Q-in-Q tags), configured IDs, VNIs (Virtual Extensible Local Area Network (VXLAN) Network Identifiers), normalized VIDs, I-SIDs (Service Instance Identifiers), etc., as long as the representation of the BDs is configured consistently across the multihomed PEs attached to that ES.
Ethernet Tag Identifier. Normalized network wide ID that is used to identify a BD within an EVI and carried in EVPN routes.

An EVPN instance spanning the Provider Edge (PE) devices participating in that EVPN. An EVI may be comprised of one BD (VLAN-based or VLAN Bundle services) or multiple BDs (VLAN-aware Bundle service).
A mechanism to efficiently and quickly signal, to remote PE nodes, the need to update their forwarding tables upon the occurrence of a failure in connectivity to an Ethernet segment.
An EVPN Instance Unicast Forwarding Table.
VLAN-ID after VLAN-ID translation (passed via MPLS).
A tunnel through the network of one or more SPs. In this document, P-tunnels are instantiated as bidirectional multicast distribution trees.

When only a single PE, among all the PEs attached to an Ethernet segment, is allowed to forward traffic to/from that Ethernet segment for a given VLAN, then the Ethernet segment is defined to be operating in Single-Active redundancy mode.
Note: This document attempts to use MACT instead of BT of [RFC7432bis]. The BT table has one key - MAC Address. The MACT table has two keys: VLAN-ID and MAC address.

Note: It is recommended that the MAC Change callback supports a small cache to drop duplicated BCM messages: If (received message is saved into the cache) Drop the message. If (there is not a free room into the cache) Delete the oldest message. Save the received message into the cache.

list_items: One or more Broadcast Domains (BDs). A BD is identified by VLAN-ID.

All-active load balancing: Supports multi-homed devices with per-flow load balancing. This mode allows the access device to connect via a "single" Ethernet bundle to multiple PEs and to send / receive traffic of the "same" VLAN from all the PEs in the same Ethernet segment.

Single-active load balancing: Supports multi-homed devices with per-vlan load balancing. In this mode, the access device connects via "separate" Ethernet bundles to multiple PEs. PE routers in turn automatically perform service carving in order to divide VLAN forwarding responsibilities across the PEs in the Ethernet segment. The access device learns via the data-plane which Ethernet bundle to use for a given VLAN.

Ethernet segment reachability: The PE discovers remote ESes and their corresponding redundancy mode (all-active or single-active). In case of segment failures, PEs withdraw the routes used at this stage in order to trigger fast convergence by signaling a MAC mass withdrawal on remote PEs.

Redundancy Group membership: PEs connected to the same Ethernet segment (multi-homing) automatically discover each other and elect a Designated Forwarder (DF) that is responsible for forwarding Broadcast, Unknown unicast and Multicast (BUM) traffic for a given EVI.

VPN membership: The PE discovers all remote PE members of a given EVI. In the case of a multicast ingress replication model, this information is used to build the PE's flood list associated with an EVI.
The Layer 3 VPN is not supported:
The following fields into the created MAC/IP Advertisement Routes are filled by 0 and are ignored in the ones received ([RFC7432bis], chapter 7.2):
Only Ingress Replication is supported, P2MP LSPs are not supported ([RFC7432bis], chapter 16).
Only Data-Plane MAC learning by software on interfaces attached to local CEs ([RFC7432bis, 14.2.1) is supported. Optional functionality described in ([RFC7432bis], chapter 10) is not supported.
The "Unique VLAN EVPN" scenario as a special case ([RFC7432bis], chapters 4, 7.9, and 7.10.1) is not supported.
Optional functionality described by ([RFC7432bis], chapters 10) is not supported.
Only ESI types 0, 1 and 4 are supported.
Only the following DF selection algorithms (see the CLI command service-carving) are supported:
VLAN-Aware Bundle EVPN evpn1 for VLAN-IDs 1-3
VLAN-Aware Bundle EVPN evpn2 for VLAN-IDs 4-5

0: Default DF election algorithm, or modulus-based algorithm ([RFC7432bis] and ([RFC8584]).

Bit 0: [Pref-DF] D bit or 'Don't Preempt' bit (DP hereafter), determines if the PE advertising the ES route requests the remote PEs in the ES not to preempt it as DF. The default value is DP=0, which is compatible with the 'preempt' or 'revertive' behavior in the Default DF Alg [RFC7432bis]. The DP capability is supported by Alg 2 and Alg 3, and MAY be used with DF Alg 0 or 1. The procedures of the DP capability for DF Alg 0 or 1 are out of the scope of [Pref-DF].
Bit 1: ([RFC8584]) AC-DF Capability (AC-Influenced DF Election, ([RFC8584], chapter 4)).

DF Preference ([Pref-DF]): defines a 2-octet value that indicates the PE preference to become the DF in the ES. The allowed values are within the range 0-65535, and the default value MUST be 32767. This value is the midpoint in the allowed Preference range of values, which gives the operator the flexibility of choosing a significant number of values, above or below the default Preference. The DF Preference field is specific to DF Alg 2 and DF Alg 3 and does not represent any Preference value for other Algs. If the DF 3 is different than Alg 2 or Alg 3, these two octets can be encoded differently.
Next Hop field of the MP_REACH_NLRI attribute = IP address of advertising PE
